q: Heat is the form of energy that can be transferred from one system to another as a result of temperature difference.
Heat transfer and thermodynamics are two complementary branches of science.
Thermodynamics deals with equilibrium states by outlining the relationship between energy and work.
One of the basic requirement for heat transfer is the presence of a temperature difference.
There can be net heat transfer between two systems that are at the same temperature.
In fluids, heat is mainly transferred by convection, in which the motion of the fluid itself carries heat from one place to another.
In solid, heat is transferred by conduction process in which heat energy is transferred among the adjacent parts of the material without the motion of the material itself.
Heat radiation occurs in the form of electromagnetic waves.
The individual parts of a medium do not move as a whole in heat conduction.
Heat energy is transferred by successive distribution among the adjacent parts in heat conduction.
Metals generally are very good conductors of heat because they have many free electrons.
The handle end of a spoon in a hot cup of tea becomes hot by successive distribution of heat energy among the adjacent parts.
The rate of heat conduction through a material of cross-sectional area $A$ is proportional to the thickness $l$ of the material.
Thermal conductivity $k$ is a measure of the ability of a material to conduct heat.
In SI units, $k$ is watt per metre per kelvin ($W m^{-1} K^{-1}$).
The ratio $\frac{T_2-T_1}{l}$ is called temperature gradient.
Fourier's law states that the rate of heat flow per unit area is proportional to the magnitude of temperature gradient.
Negative sign in Fourier's law indicates that temperature $T$ must decrease as distance $x$ increases.
In the SI units, heat flux density is watt per metre squared ($W m^{-2}$).
Heat current $H$ is measured in watt ($W$).
A high value for thermal conductivity indicates that the material is a good thermal insulator.
Materials such as copper and silver that are good electric conductors are also good heat conductors.
Rubber, wood, and styrofoam are poor conductors of heat and have low conductivity values.
Heat convection is the flow of heat through a fluid from places of higher temperature to places of lower temperature by movement of the fluid itself.
Convection currents are set up in the fluid because the hotter part of the fluid is not as dense as the cooler part.
Convection occurs only in a liquid or a gas, never in a solid.
Buoyant force is what drives natural convection.
The basic equation for the rate of convection heat transfer is known as Newton's law of cooling.
In the equation $H_{conv} = q A (T_2-T_1)$, $q$ is the convective heat transfer coefficient.
In SI units, $q$ is expressed watt per metre squared per kelvin ($W m^{-2} K^{-1}$).
When $T_2 < T_1$, heat is gained by the object and $H_{conv}$ is positive.
Heat radiation is a transfer of heat that does not require a material medium.
Energy transfer by radiation is the fastest (at the speed of light) and it suffers no attenuation in a vacuum.
All bodies at a temperature above absolute zero emit thermal radiation.
Thermal radiation differs from other forms of electromagnetic radiation such as X-rays that are not related to temperature.
Thermodynamics is the study of systems involving energy in the form of heat and work.
When two systems are in thermal equilibrium, there is no net heat transfer between them.
Systems at the same temperature will be in thermal equilibrium with each other.
The zeroth law of thermodynamics states that when two objects are separately in thermodynamic equilibrium with a third object, they all are in thermodynamic equilibrium with each other.
The first law of thermodynamics is an application of the conservation energy for heat and thermodynamic processes.
The change in internal energy $\Delta U$ of a system is equal to the net heat flow $Q$ into the system minus the total work $W$ done by the system.
$Q$ is positive if heat is added to the system, and negative if heat is removed.
$W$ is positive if work is done by the system, and negative if work is done on the system.
The internal energy $U$ of a system is defined as the sum of the energies of all the constituent particles.
The internal energy of ideal gases depends only on temperature, not on volume or pressure.
The internal energy of an ideal gas is directly proportional to the absolute temperature.
Heat transfer and work are both stored as such in a system.
Internal energy is a form of energy completely different from either heat or work.
A thermodynamic process may be defined as a change from one equilibrium state of a system to another.
In thermodynamics, a quasi-static process is a process that happens slowly enough for the system to remain in internal physical thermodynamic equilibrium.
An isobaric process occurs at constant pressure.
The work done by the system in an isobaric process is simply the pressure multiplied by the change in volume.
In an isobaric process, the work is equal to the area of the shaded rectangle on a pressure Vs volume graph.
An isochoric process occurs at constant volume.
In an isochoric process, no work is done by the system because $W = p \Delta V = 0$.
According to the first law, the change in internal energy must equal any heat flowing into or out of the system in an isochoric process.
$c_v$ is specific heat capacity at constant volume.
$C_V$ is molar heat capacity at constant volume.
For an ideal monatomic gas, the change in internal energy is expressed as $\Delta U = \frac{3}{2} n R \Delta T$.
An isothermal process occurs at a constant temperature (i.e. $\Delta T = 0$).
For a process to be isothermal, any heat flow into or out of the system must occur slowly to maintain thermodynamic equilibrium.
For an ideal gas, internal energy $U$ is only a function of the temperature.
For an isothermal process, $\Delta U$ is equal to zero.
In an isothermal process, $W = Q$.
If the system does work in an isothermal process, the energy comes from heat flowing into the system from the reservoir.
In an adiabatic process, there is no heat transfer into or out of the system, i.e. $Q = 0$.
An adiabatic process can be done by enclosing the system in a thermal insulator (adiabatic container).
For an adiabatic process, the first law of thermodynamics is reduced to $\Delta U = -W$.
$\gamma$ is the ratio of the heat capacities, $\gamma = \frac{C_p}{C_v}$.
The temperature of a gas increases when it is adiabatically compressed.
Heat engine is a system that converts heat to mechanical energy, which can then be used to do mechanical work.
A series of processes in which a system is brought back to its initial state is called a cycle.
Thermal efficiency for a heat engine is the ratio of work output to heat input.
Applying the first law of thermodynamic to one complete cycle, there is no net change of internal energy ($\Delta U = 0$).
For a heat engine cycle, $Q_H - Q_C = W$.
Internal combustion engines generate the input heat by the combustion of fuel within the engine itself.
A gasoline engine is called a four stroke engine.
The gasoline engine cycle involves the performance of six processes, of which four require motion of the piston.
In the intake stroke, a mixture of gasoline vapour and air is drawn into the cylinder by the suction stroke of the piston.
In the compression stroke, the mixture of gasoline vapour and air is compressed until its pressure and temperature rise considerably.
Explosion of the hot mixture is caused to take place very rapidly by an electric spark.
The piston does not move during the explosion process.
During explosion, the combustion products attain a very high pressure and temperature, but the volume remains unchanged.
In the power stroke, the hot combustion products expand and push the piston out, thus suffering a drop in pressure and temperature.
An exhaust valve allows some gas to escape during valve exhaust until the pressure drops to that of the atmosphere.
In the exhaust stroke, the piston pushes almost all the remaining combustion products out of the cylinder.
The ratio $\frac{V_1}{V_2}$ is the compression ratio.
For a typical ideal gasoline engine with a compression ratio of 8 and $\gamma = 1.4$, the theoretical efficiency is $\eta = 56\%$.
The diesel engine induces only air into the combustion chamber on its intake stroke.
In a diesel engine, fuel is sprayed into the cylinder and immediately ignites because of the heat and pressure inside.
The diesel engine can operate on a two-stroke or four-stroke cycle.
Steam engine is operating between the steam temperature 373 K and outside room temperature 300 K.
Gasoline engine is operating between petrol burning temperature 680 K and outside room temperature 300 K.
Diesel engine is operating between the diesel burning temperature 873 K and outside room temperature 300 K.
Nicolas Leonard Sadi Carnot described a hypothetical engine known as Carnot engine.
The Carnot engine operates on an ideal reversible cycle between two heat reservoirs.
Carnot engine sets an upper limit on the efficiency of all other real engines.
Real engines are less efficient than Carnot engine because they do not operate through a reversible cycle.
Efficiency of real engines is further reduced due to friction and energy losses by conduction.
In a reversible process, the system undergoing the process can be returned to its initial condition along the same path on the p-V diagram.
The most important condition for a process to be reversible is that the process should be carried out infinitesimally slowly.
The Carnot cycle consists of four reversible processes; two isothermal processes and two adiabatic processes.
Step 1 of the Carnot cycle involves reversible isothermal expansion of the gas at the hot temperature $T_H$.
Step 2 of the Carnot cycle involves reversible adiabatic expansion, during which the gas cools to the cold temperature $T_C$.
Carnot's theorem states that no engine operating between two heat reservoirs can be more efficient than a Carnot engine operating between the same reservoirs.
